problems.title: Container Deposit Refund
problems.prompt:
A recycling depot pays 10 cents for containers holding at most one litre and 25 cents for larger containers.

Background and required method:
Both counts must be non-negative integers. Calculate the refund in dollars and round it to two decimal places.

Implement this interface:
def deposit_refund(small: int, large: int) -> float:

Parameters:
• small (int): Number of containers holding one litre or less.
• large (int): Number of containers holding more than one litre.

Expected result:
The refund amount in dollars, rounded to two decimals.
problems.signature: def deposit_refund(small: int, large: int) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def deposit_refund(small: int, large: int) -> float:
    """Calculate the refund for returned drink containers.

    Args:
        small (int): Number of containers holding one litre or less.
        large (int): Number of containers holding more than one litre.

    Returns:
        float: The refund amount in dollars, rounded to two decimals.
    """
    pass

problems.title: Tax and Tip
problems.prompt:
A restaurant calculates both sales tax and a tip from the meal's original price.

Background and required method:
Use the supplied decimal rates, not percentages. Round each monetary result to two decimal places before returning it.

Implement this interface:
def restaurant_total(meal_cost: float, tax_rate: float = 0.05, tip_rate: float = 0.18) -> tuple[float, float, float]:

Parameters:
• meal_cost (float): Pre-tax cost of the meal in dollars.
• tax_rate (float): Sales-tax rate as a decimal.
• tip_rate (float): Tip rate as a decimal.

Expected result:
The tax, tip, and final total in dollars.
problems.signature: def restaurant_total(meal_cost: float, tax_rate: float = 0.05, tip_rate: float = 0.18) -> tuple[float, float, float]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def restaurant_total(meal_cost: float, tax_rate: float = 0.05, tip_rate: float = 0.18) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
    """Calculate tax, tip, and the final restaurant bill.

    Args:
        meal_cost (float): Pre-tax cost of the meal in dollars.
        tax_rate (float): Sales-tax rate as a decimal.
        tip_rate (float): Tip rate as a decimal.

    Returns:
        tuple[float, float, float]: The tax, tip, and final total in dollars.
    """
    pass

problems.title: Sum the First n Integers
problems.prompt:
The sequence 1 + 2 + ... + n has a direct arithmetic formula.

Background and required method:
Use n * (n + 1) // 2 rather than a loop. n is a non-negative integer.

Implement this interface:
def sum_to_n(n: int) -> int:

Parameters:
• n (int): Inclusive upper bound of the sequence.

Expected result:
The sum from 1 through n; zero when n is zero.
problems.signature: def sum_to_n(n: int) -> int:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def sum_to_n(n: int) -> int:
    """Calculate the sum of the positive integers from 1 through n.

    Args:
        n (int): Inclusive upper bound of the sequence.

    Returns:
        int: The sum from 1 through n; zero when n is zero.
    """
    pass

problems.title: Widget Shipment Weight
problems.prompt:
Each widget weighs 75 grams and each gizmo weighs 112 grams.

Multiply each non-negative item count by its unit weight and add the results.

def shipment_weight(widgets: int, gizmos: int) -> int:

Parameters:
• widgets (int): Number of 75-gram widgets.
• gizmos (int): Number of 112-gram gizmos.

Expected result:
Total shipment weight in grams.
problems.signature: def shipment_weight(widgets: int, gizmos: int) -> int:

    Returns:
        int: Total shipment weight in grams.

problems.title: Compound Interest Forecast
problems.prompt:
Interest is added to the account once per year, and the next year's interest is calculated from the new balance.

Background and required method:
Return one rounded balance for each year. The default annual rate is 4 percent and years must be non-negative.

Implement this interface:
def account_balances(principal: float, years: int = 3, rate: float = 0.04) -> list[float]:

Parameters:
• principal (float): Initial account balance.
• years (int): Number of annual balances to calculate.
• rate (float): Annual interest rate as a decimal.

Expected result:
Year-end balances rounded to two decimal places.
problems.signature: def account_balances(principal: float, years: int = 3, rate: float = 0.04) -> list[float]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def account_balances(principal: float, years: int = 3, rate: float = 0.04) -> list[float]:
    """Project an account balance over several years of annual compounding.

    Args:
        principal (float): Initial account balance.
        years (int): Number of annual balances to calculate.
        rate (float): Annual interest rate as a decimal.

    Returns:
        list[float]: Year-end balances rounded to two decimal places.
    """
    pass

problems.title: Arithmetic Report
problems.prompt:
A compact arithmetic report is useful for practising Python's basic numeric operators.

Background and required method:
Return sum, first-minus-second, product, quotient, remainder, log10 of the first value, and first raised to the second. The first value must be positive and the second non-zero.

Implement this interface:
def arithmetic_report(first: int, second: int) -> tuple[float, ...]:

Parameters:
• first (int): Positive first integer.
• second (int): Non-zero second integer.

Expected result:
Seven results in the order stated in the task.
problems.signature: def arithmetic_report(first: int, second: int) -> tuple[float, ...]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import math

def arithmetic_report(first: int, second: int) -> tuple[float, ...]:
    """Calculate a collection of arithmetic results for two integers.

    Args:
        first (int): Positive first integer.
        second (int): Non-zero second integer.

    Returns:
        tuple[float, ...]: Seven results in the order stated in the task.
    """
    pass

problems.title: Fuel Efficiency Converter
problems.prompt:
Canada commonly reports fuel consumption in L/100 km while the United States often uses miles per gallon.

Background and required method:
Use the conversion 235.215 / mpg. mpg must be positive.

Implement this interface:
def mpg_to_l_per_100km(mpg: float) -> float:

Parameters:
• mpg (float): Fuel efficiency in US miles per gallon.

Expected result:
Equivalent litres consumed per 100 kilometres.
problems.signature: def mpg_to_l_per_100km(mpg: float) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def mpg_to_l_per_100km(mpg: float) -> float:
    """Convert miles per gallon to litres per 100 kilometres.

    Args:
        mpg (float): Fuel efficiency in US miles per gallon.

    Returns:
        float: Equivalent litres consumed per 100 kilometres.
    """
    pass

tests.name: thirty mpg
tests.code:
actual = mpg_to_l_per_100km(30)
expected = 7.8405
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: efficient vehicle
tests.code:
actual = mpg_to_l_per_100km(50)
expected = 4.7043
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: one mpg
tests.code:
actual = mpg_to_l_per_100km(1)
expected = 235.215
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Distance Across Earth
problems.prompt:
Locations on Earth lie on a sphere, so straight Cartesian distance is not appropriate.

Background and required method:
Convert all angles from degrees to radians and use the spherical law of cosines with Earth radius 6371.01 km.

Implement this interface:
def earth_distance(lat1: float, lon1: float, lat2: float, lon2: float) -> float:

Parameters:
• lat1 (float): First latitude in degrees.
• lon1 (float): First longitude in degrees.
• lat2 (float): Second latitude in degrees.
• lon2 (float): Second longitude in degrees.

Expected result:
Great-circle distance in kilometres.
problems.signature: def earth_distance(lat1: float, lon1: float, lat2: float, lon2: float) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import math

def earth_distance(lat1: float, lon1: float, lat2: float, lon2: float) -> float:
    """Calculate great-circle distance between two latitude-longitude points.

    Args:
        lat1 (float): First latitude in degrees.
        lon1 (float): First longitude in degrees.
        lat2 (float): Second latitude in degrees.
        lon2 (float): Second longitude in degrees.

    Returns:
        float: Great-circle distance in kilometres.
    """
    pass

tests.name: same point
tests.code:
actual = earth_distance(10, 20, 10, 20)
expected = 0.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: one degree longitude
tests.code:
actual = earth_distance(0, 0, 0, 1)
expected = 111.19492664455873
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: quarter circumference
tests.code:
actual = earth_distance(0, 0, 0, 90)
expected = 10007.558398010286
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Make Change
problems.prompt:
A greedy change calculation repeatedly uses the largest coin that does not exceed the remaining amount.

Use denominations 200, 100, 25, 10, 5, and 1 cents, in that order. Return every denomination as a dictionary key, including zero counts.

Implement this interface:
def make_change(cents: int) -> dict[int, int]:

• cents (int): Non-negative amount to decompose.

Coin counts keyed by denomination in descending order.
problems.signature: def make_change(cents: int) -> dict[int, int]:

    """

problems.title: Height Unit Converter
problems.prompt:
A foot contains 12 inches and one inch is exactly 2.54 centimetres.

Background and required method:
Combine the two measurements before converting. Both inputs must be non-negative.

Implement this interface:
def height_in_cm(feet: int, inches: float) -> float:

Parameters:
• feet (int): Whole feet in the height.
• inches (float): Additional inches in the height.

Expected result:
Total height in centimetres.
problems.signature: def height_in_cm(feet: int, inches: float) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:

    Args:
        feet (int): Whole feet in the height.
        inches (float): Additional inches in the height.

    Returns:
        float: Total height in centimetres.
    """
    pass

tests.name: five foot eight
tests.code:
actual = height_in_cm(5, 8)
expected = 172.72
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: one foot
tests.code:

expected = 30.48
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: inches only
tests.code:

expected = 25.4
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Circle Area and Sphere Volume
problems.prompt:
The area of a circle is pi*r^2 and the volume of a sphere is 4*pi*r^3/3.

Background and required method:
Use math.pi and a non-negative radius.

Implement this interface:
def circle_and_sphere(radius: float) -> tuple[float, float]:

Parameters:
• radius (float): Radius of both shapes.

Circle area followed by sphere volume.

def circle_and_sphere(radius: float) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Calculate measurements associated with a radius.

    Args:
        radius (float): Radius of both shapes.

    Returns:
        tuple[float, float]: Circle area followed by sphere volume.

    pass

tests.name: radius one area
tests.code:

tests.name: radius one volume
tests.code:

expected = 4.1887902047863905

problems.title: Heating Water
problems.prompt:
Water has a specific heat capacity of 4.186 joules per gram per degree Celsius. One kilowatt-hour is 3,600,000 joules.

Background and required method:
Compute q = mass * 4.186 * temperature_change and then price the energy using cents_per_kwh.

Implement this interface:
def heating_energy(mass_grams: float, temperature_change: float, cents_per_kwh: float = 8.9) -> tuple[float, float]:

Parameters:
• mass_grams (float): Mass of water in grams.
• temperature_change (float): Increase in degrees Celsius.
• cents_per_kwh (float): Electricity price in cents per kilowatt-hour.

Expected result:
Required energy in joules and cost in cents.
problems.signature: def heating_energy(mass_grams: float, temperature_change: float, cents_per_kwh: float = 8.9) -> tuple[float, float]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def heating_energy(mass_grams: float, temperature_change: float, cents_per_kwh: float = 8.9) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Calculate energy and electricity cost for heating water.

    Args:
        mass_grams (float): Mass of water in grams.
        temperature_change (float): Increase in degrees Celsius.
        cents_per_kwh (float): Electricity price in cents per kilowatt-hour.

    Returns:
        tuple[float, float]: Required energy in joules and cost in cents.
    """
    pass

tests.name: energy
tests.code:
actual = heating_energy(1000, 10)[0]
expected = 41860.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: cost
tests.code:
actual = heating_energy(1000, 10)[1]
expected = 0.10348166666666667
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Free-Fall Speed
problems.prompt:
Ignoring air resistance, final speed follows v = sqrt(2*g*d), where g is gravitational acceleration.

Background and required method:
Use g = 9.8 m/s^2 by default and require non-negative distance.

Implement this interface:
def impact_speed(distance: float, gravity: float = 9.8) -> float:

Parameters:
• distance (float): Falling distance in metres.
• gravity (float): Gravitational acceleration in m/s^2.

Expected result:
Impact speed in metres per second.
problems.signature: def impact_speed(distance: float, gravity: float = 9.8) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import math

def impact_speed(distance: float, gravity: float = 9.8) -> float:
    """Calculate impact speed for an object dropped from rest.

    Args:
        distance (float): Falling distance in metres.
        gravity (float): Gravitational acceleration in m/s^2.

    Returns:
        float: Impact speed in metres per second.
    """
    pass

tests.name: twenty metres
tests.code:
actual = impact_speed(20)
expected = 19.79898987322333
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: zero distance
tests.code:
actual = impact_speed(0)
expected = 0.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: custom gravity
tests.code:
actual = impact_speed(8, 10)
expected = 12.649110640673518
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Ideal Gas Amount
problems.prompt:
The ideal gas law is PV = nRT. Pressure is provided in pascals and volume in litres, which must be converted to cubic metres.

Background and required method:
Rearrange to n = P*V/(R*T), use R = 8.314 J/(mol*K), and require positive temperature.

Implement this interface:
def gas_moles(pressure_pa: float, volume_litres: float, temperature_k: float) -> float:

Parameters:
• pressure_pa (float): Gas pressure in pascals.
• volume_litres (float): Gas volume in litres.
• temperature_k (float): Absolute temperature in kelvin.

Expected result:
Amount of gas in moles.
problems.signature: def gas_moles(pressure_pa: float, volume_litres: float, temperature_k: float) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def gas_moles(pressure_pa: float, volume_litres: float, temperature_k: float) -> float:
    """Calculate the amount of gas using the ideal gas law.

    Args:
        pressure_pa (float): Gas pressure in pascals.
        volume_litres (float): Gas volume in litres.
        temperature_k (float): Absolute temperature in kelvin.

    Returns:
        float: Amount of gas in moles.
    """
    pass

tests.name: standard conditions
tests.code:
actual = gas_moles(101325, 22.4, 273.15)
expected = 0.9993767399537038
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: scaled volume
tests.code:
actual = gas_moles(101325, 44.8, 273.15)
expected = 1.9987534799074076
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: simple values
tests.code:
actual = gas_moles(8314, 1, 1)
expected = 1.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: scales linearly with pressure
tests.code:
base = gas_moles(100000, 10, 300)
doubled = gas_moles(200000, 10, 300)
assert abs(doubled - 2 * base) < 1e-12

A triangle occupies half of the rectangle with the same base and perpendicular height.

Use area = base * height / 2 with non-negative measurements.

problems.title: Triangle Area from Three Sides
problems.prompt:
When all three side lengths are known, let s be half their sum and calculate sqrt(s*(s-a)*(s-b)*(s-c)).

Background and required method:
The supplied sides form a valid non-degenerate triangle.

Implement this interface:
def heron_area(a: float, b: float, c: float) -> float:

Parameters:
• a (float): First side length.
• b (float): Second side length.
• c (float): Third side length.

Expected result:
Triangle area in square units.
problems.signature: def heron_area(a: float, b: float, c: float) -> float:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import math

def heron_area(a: float, b: float, c: float) -> float:
    """Calculate a triangle's area with Heron's formula.

    Args:
        a (float): First side length.
        b (float): Second side length.
        c (float): Third side length.

    Returns:
        float: Triangle area in square units.
    """
    pass

tests.name: right triangle
tests.code:
actual = heron_area(3, 4, 5)
expected = 6.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: equilateral
tests.code:
actual = heron_area(2, 2, 2)
expected = 1.7320508075688772
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: larger triangle
tests.code:
actual = heron_area(5, 5, 6)
expected = 12.0
assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

problems.title: Regular Polygon Area
problems.prompt:
A regular polygon has n equal sides of length s and area n*s^2 / (4*tan(pi/n)).

Background and required method:
Use math.pi and math.tan. n is at least 3 and side_length is positive.

Implement this interface:
def regular_polygon_area(sides: int, side_length: float) -> float:

• sides (int): Number of equal polygon sides.
• side_length (float): Length of each side.

Polygon area in square units.

problems.title: Format a Unix Timestamp
problems.prompt:
Unix timestamps count seconds from 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC. Accepting the timestamp as an argument keeps the function deterministic and testable.

Background and required method:
Use datetime.fromtimestamp with timezone.utc and return ISO-like YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC text.

Implement this interface:
def format_unix_time(timestamp: int | float) -> str:

Parameters:
• timestamp (int | float): Seconds since the Unix epoch.

Expected result:
UTC date and time in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC format.

tests.name: sample adult

actual = body_mass_index(70, 1.75)

assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'

tests.name: unit values

assert abs(actual - expected) < 1e-9, f'Expected {expected}, got {actual}.'
